a|true|college578.txt.passage:
(1) Governments around the world are struggling to cope with the rising cost of health care, and of drugs in particular.
(2) Many rich countries have resorted to price controls, and some on the American left advocate them noisily.
(3) But drug firms maintain that America, where they are free to price patented pills largely as they please, is the engine of global pharmaceutical innovation, while price-controlling Europeans are free riders.
(4) That, says PhRMA, the industry's lobby based in Washington, D.C., is because price regulations seen in other rich countries" chill innovation, impede patients' access to the newest cutting-edge medicines, and trigger innovators to relocate to countries with more progressive public policy."
(5) A new study, written by Donald Light, a visiting professor at Stanford University, claims that European drug firms are more innovative than American ones, in spite of price controls.
(6) That flies in the face of an influential paper published in the same journal in 2006, which examined the geographic origins of drugs registered between 1982 and 2003 and concluded that favourable public policies had helped propel America to the top of the list.
(7) PhRMA has rushed to denounce Mr Light's study, insisting it gives a" distorted picture"  and understates the impact of" home-grown innovation" .
(8) The tiff exposes two fallacies that come from looking at a globalised industry through national spectacles.
(9) Both sides attribute new drugs to a specific country of origin (based on the location of the headquarters of the firm that first launches them).
(10) But Patricia Danzon of the Wharton Business School argues that this makes little sense when most big drug firms have laboratories in several countries and often acquire drugs under development from biotechnology firms located elsewhere.
(11) By the same token, firms develop drugs for the global market, observes Alan Garber of Stanford, not just for the local one.
(12) So the imposition of price caps in a given country will not necessarily quash innovation there, thanks to the lure of exports.
(13) The exception to this rule, of course, is the United States, which alone accounts for over 40% of global sales.
(14) So what would happen if America's Congress imposes price regulations?
(15) If the cuts are swinging, then the prize for inventors everywhere will be reduced.
(16) But short of such a radical scenario, pricing reform would not kill off innovation, according to Sanford Bernstein, a financial-research firm.
(17) It has analysed the likely impact of a cut of 20% in the prices Medicare, the government health scheme for the elderly, pays for its drugs.
(18) It concluded that the earnings per share of big pharmaceutical firms would drop by 3-8%.
(19) There is even reason to think that pricing reforms may boost innovation.
(20) Britain and Germany are pioneering comparative reviews of drugs' effectiveness and cost-benefit analyses aimed at reimbursing firms for new drugs based on how well they perform.
(21) Janssen-Cilag persuaded Britain's health service to accept Velcade, its expensive cancer drug, by offering a money-back guarantee if it did not work as well as promised.
(22) The American pharmaceutical lobby is violently opposed to making such an approach compulsory.
(23) Some suspect that its opposition stems from the fear that many expensive and profitable pills would be found to be of dubious value.
(24) But as Ms Danzon points out, " Comparative-effectiveness reviews are an indirect form of price control-but one that is consistent with encouraging innovation."

d1|true|college578.txt.statement: The price-control-for-innovation approach is not applicable to US.

Step-by-step reasoning:

   - The statement claims that the price-control-for-innovation approach is not applicable to the US. This suggests that imposing price controls in the US would not follow the same principles or outcomes as in other countries.

2. Retrieve Relevant Information from the Passage
   - Sentence (13): "The exception to this rule, of course, is the United States, which alone accounts for over 40% of global sales." → highlights the unique position of the US in the global pharmaceutical market.
   - Sentence (15): "If the cuts are swinging, then the prize for inventors everywhere will be reduced." → implies that significant price reductions in the US would affect global innovation incentives.
   - Sentence (16): "But short of such a radical scenario, pricing reform would not kill off innovation, according to Sanford Bernstein, a financial-research firm." → suggests that moderate pricing reforms might not harm innovation.
   - Sentence (18): "It concluded that the earnings per share of big pharmaceutical firms would drop by 3-8%." → quantifies the impact of a moderate price reduction on earnings.
   - Sentence (19): "There is even reason to think that pricing reforms may boost innovation." → indicates that pricing reforms could potentially enhance innovation.

   - Sentence (13) emphasizes the unique role of the US in the global pharmaceutical market, which could imply that the US market dynamics differ from those in other countries.
   - Sentences (15) and (16) suggest that while significant price reductions could reduce incentives for innovation globally, moderate reforms might not have such a drastic effect.
   - Sentence (18) provides a specific example of the impact of a moderate price reduction, showing that it would not severely harm the financial performance of pharmaceutical companies.
   - Sentence (19) further supports the idea that pricing reforms could potentially enhance innovation, contrary to the notion that price controls stifle innovation.

   - Sentence (13) alone highlights the unique position of the US but does not directly address the applicability of price controls.
   - Sentences (15) and (16) together indicate that moderate pricing reforms would not necessarily harm innovation, which counters the idea that price controls are not applicable.
   - Sentence (19) further supports the notion that pricing reforms could potentially enhance innovation, reinforcing the idea that price controls can be applied effectively.
   - Therefore, sentences (15), (16), and (19) collectively provide a comprehensive argument that contradicts the statement.

5. Anaphora Resolution
   - No anaphoric expressions are present in the relevant sentences that require additional context to resolve.

6. Conclusion
   - Sentences (15), (16), and (19) together provide a comprehensive argument that contradicts the statement that the price-control-for-innovation approach is not applicable to the US.
   - These sentences collectively show that moderate pricing reforms would not necessarily harm innovation and could potentially enhance it.

a|true|college543.txt.passage:
(1) The American military tribunals set up to pass judgment on terrorism suspects at Guantánamo Bay lurched into life this week when David Hicks, the" Australian Taliban" , unexpectedly pleaded guilty.
(2) The Pentagon will be relieved that the tribunals have started to show results after five years of controversy over the status of" enemy combatants" , claims of torture, the admissibility of forced confessions and a Supreme Court ruling last year that halted an earlier version of the tribunals.
(3) Yet the Hicks case is hardly an impressive start for America's offshore justice.
(4) Critics say the 31-year-old Mr Hicks, a former kangaroo skinner and alleged al-Qaeda fighter, faces a" kangaroo court" .
(5) Two of his civilian lawyers were barred from the hearing on March 26th.
(6) His military lawyer, Major Michael Mori, has been threatened with prosecution for harshly criticising the tribunals.
(7) The prosecutor, Colonel Morris Davis, said the defence lawyer may have breached military law that bans officers from using" contemptuous words"  against the president or senior officials.
(8) Major Mori filed a counter-charge against the prosecution, saying it was trying to intimidate him.
(9) In the end the motion will not be heard because Mr Hicks, looking pale and bedraggled, admitted the charge of" providing material support for terrorism" .
(10) A charge of" attempted murder in violation of the laws of war"  was dropped.
(11) As The Economist went to press, the tribunal was due to hear details of his plea and pass sentence, which Mr Hicks is expected to serve out in Australia.
(12) His father, Terry Hicks, said his son had had" five years of absolute hell"  since being captured in Afghanistan and allegedly suffering beatings, rape and forced injections in American custody-accusations rejected by the Pentagon.
(13) The guilty plea was just" a way to get home" , said Mr Hicks's father.
(14) Many in Australia regard Mr Hicks as more of a lost soul than a dangerous terrorist.
(15) Indeed, his charge sheet portrays him as little more than an al-Qaeda foot-soldier, and a poor one at that.
(16) His jihadi CV is pitiful compared with the evidence being given by some of the 14" high value detainees"  belatedly brought to Guantánamo from CIA secret prisons in September.
(17) They include al-Qaeda's operational chief, Khalid Sheikh Mohammed, who in a closed hearing to determine whether he was an" enemy combatant"  earlier this month boasted that he had organised the September 11th attacks" from A to Z" , and 30 other plots.
(18) Meanwhile, Ahmed Ghalfan Ghailani and Waleed bin Atttash, from Tanzania and Yemen respectively, have admitted supplying equipment for the bombing of the American embassies in Kenya and Tanzania in 1998.
(19) Many of these big names will face trial, and perhaps the death penalty.
(20) But the tribunals are expected to try only 60-80 of the more than 380 prisoners currently in Guantánamo.
(21) Over the years hundreds of detainees have been sent back to their countries, where some have been jailed and most have been released-and sometimes re-arrested.
(22) The Bush Administration says it wants to phase out and eventually close the jail.
(23) But it is unlikely to do so for some years, either because it lacks the evidence to prosecute detainees (even under the more lax military rules it is using), or because other countries are unwilling to take them back.

a|true|college561.txt.passage:
(1) There was a time when big-league university presidents really mattered.
(2) The New York Times covered their every move.
(3) Presidents, the real ones, sought their counsel.
(4) For Woodrow Wilson and Dwight Eisenhower, being head of Princeton and Columbia, respectively, was a stepping-stone to the White House.
(5) Today, though, the job of college president is less and less removed from that of the Avon lady (except the house calls are made to the doorsteps of wealthy alumni).
(6) Ruth Simmons, the newly installed president of Brown University and the first African American to lead an Ivy League school, is a throwback to the crusading campus leaders of old.
(7) She doesn't merely marshal funds; she invests them in the great educational causes of our day.
(8) With the more than $300 million she raised as president of Smith College from 1995 to 2001, Simmons established an engineering program  (the first at any women's school) and added seminars focused on public speaking to purge the ubiquitous" likes"  and" ums"  from the campus idiom.
(9) At a meeting to discuss the future of Smith's math department, one professor timidly requested two more discussion sections for his course.
(10) Her response:" Dream bigger."
(11) Her own dream was born in a sharecropper's shack in East Texas where there was no money for books or toys-she and her 11 siblings each got an apple, an orange and 10 nuts for Christmas.
(12) Though she was called nigger on her walk to school, entering the classroom, she says, " was like waking up."
(13) When Simmons won a scholarship to Dillard University, her high school teachers took up a collection so she'd have a coat.
(14) She went on to Harvard to earn a Ph.
(15) D. in Romance languages.
(16) Simmons has made diversity her No.
(17) 1 campus crusade.
(18) She nearly doubled the enrollment of black freshmen at Smith, largely by traveling to high schools in the nation's poorest ZIP codes to recruit.
(19) Concerned with the lives of minority students once they arrive at school, she has fought to ease the racial standoffs that plague so many campuses.
(20) At Smith she turned down a request by students to have race-specific dorms.
(21) In 1993, while vice provost at Princeton, she wrote a now famous report recommending that the university establish an office of conflict resolution to defuse racial misunderstandings before they boiled over.
(22) Her first task at Brown will be to heal one such rupture last spring after the student paper published an incendiary ad by conservative polemicist David Horowitz arguing that blacks economically benefited from slavery."
(23) There's no safe ground for anybody in race relations, but campuses, unlike any other institution in our society, provide the opportunity to cross racial lines,"  says Simmons. "

(1) As long as her parents can remember, 13-year-old Katie Hart has been talking about going to college.

(7) Even a number cruncher like Tally admits it's a little scary, especially since she'll retire and Katie will go to college at around the same time.
(8) Paying for college has always been a humbling endeavor.
(9) The good news: last year students collected $74 billion in financial aid, the most ever.
(10) Most families pay less than full freight.
(11) Sixty percent of public-university students and three quarters of those at private colleges receive some form of financial aid-mostly, these days, in the form of loans.
(12) But those numbers are not as encouraging as they appear for lower-income families, because schools are changing their formulas for distributing aid.
(13) Eager to boost their magazine rankings, which are based in part on the test scores of entering freshmen, they're throwing more aid at smarter kids-whether they need it or not.
(14) The best way to prepare is to start saving early.

(16) So-called 529 plans allow parents to sock away funds in federal-tax-free-investment accounts, as long as the money is used for" qualified education expenses"  like tuition, room and board.
(17) The plans aren't for everyone.
(18) For tax reasons, some lower-and middle-income families may be better off choosing other investments.
(19) But saving is vital.
(20) When's the best time to start?"
(21) Sometime,"  says Jack Joyce of the College Board, " between the maternity ward and middle school."
(22) Aid packages usually come in some combination of grants, loans and jobs.
(23) These days 60 percent of all aid comes in the form of low-interest loans.
(24) All students are eligible for" unsubsidized"  federal Stafford loans, which let them defer interest payments until after graduation.
(25) Students who can demonstrate need can also qualify for federal Perkins loans or" subsidized"  Staffords, where the government pays the interest during school.
(26) Fortunately, this is a borrower's market. "
(27) Interest rates are at their lowest level in the history of student loans,"  says Mark Kantrowitz, publisher of Finaid.
(28) Kantrowitz expects rates to fall even further when they're reviewed this summer.
(29) Traditional scholarships, academic or athletic, are still a part of many families' planning.
(30) Mack Reiter, a 17-year-old national wrestling champion, gets so many recruiting letters he throws most away.
(31) He'll almost certainly get a free ride.
(32) Without it, " we would really be in a bind,"  says his mother, Janet.

(5) Therefore, even with today's relatively high standard of living, about 10 percent of the people in the United States are below the poverty line.
(6) However, if these people had stable jobs, they could have an acceptable standard of living.
(7) Economists suggest several reasons why poor people do not have jobs.
(8) For one thing, more than half of the poor people in the United States are not qualified to work.
(9) Over 40 percent of the poor people are children.
(10) By law, children less than 16 years old cannot work in many industries.
(11) A large number of poor people are old.
(12) Many companies do not hire people over 65 years old, the normal retirement age.
(13) Some poor adults do not look for jobs for a variety of personal reasons: they are sick, they do not have any motivation.
(14) they have family problems, or they do notbelieve that they can find a job.
(15) Other poor people look for a job but cannot find one.
(16) Many poor adults never went to high school.
(17) Therefore, when they look for jobs, they have few skills that they can offer.
(18) At the present time, the government thinks it can reduce poverty in the country in the following ways.
(19) First, if the national economy grows, businesses and industries hire more workers.
(20) Some of the poor who are qualified to look for jobs may find employment.
(21) Then they will no longer be below the poverty line.
(22) Second, if society invests in the poor, the poor will become more productive.
(23) If the government spends money on social programs, education, and training for poor people, the poor will have the skills to offer.
(24) Then it is more likely that they can find jobs.
(25) Finally, if the government distributes society's income differently, it raises some poor people above the poverty line.
(26) The government collects taxes from the non-poor and gives money to the poor.
(27) These payments to the poor are called welfare.
(28) In 1975 over 18 million people in the United States received welfare.
(29) Some economists are looking for better solutions to the poverty problem.
(30) However, at the present time, many people depend on welfare for a minimally acceptable standard of living.
